entities.label: Treichel 1896 Sir Cleges parallel edition and study

For the Edinburgh column, Treichel identifies Weber's 1810 edition, lists its inaccurate or altered readings with manuscript readings in parentheses, and omits Weber's modern terminal supplement. He does not identify how those manuscript readings were obtained, so direct Treichel autopsy is not asserted.

reviewNotes: The four medieval works, printed editions, physical witnesses, working transcripts, catalogs, photographic surrogates, modern digital encodings, supporting scholarship, and repository artifact are separate graph layers.
McKnight's rotographs are proof-collation sources only on the available method statement; the continuous transcript or compositor copy for the three principal texts remains unresolved.

Treichel's Oxford and Edinburgh columns have asymmetric transmission paths: only Oxford is routed through the Kaluza/Bülbring working transcript; Edinburgh is routed through Weber's 1810 print with a separately supported manuscript-comparison edge of unidentified method.
Weber's bracketed printed lines 533–540 are a modern editorial supplement and are excluded from every manuscript-transcription scope.

The NLS catalog expression and Treichel's German-only dissertation issue are modeled separately from the physical witness, image facsimile, and journal edition that actually prints the Middle English text.
Dame Siriz and the Interludium are related analogues, but no direct derivation relation between their surviving texts is asserted.
Rights statements apply only to the named entity or component. Public-domain medieval text does not make modern manuscript photography unrestricted.
The ignored source cache retains whole manuscripts and complete printed source objects where available; local work_portion locators identify the relevant portions without implying cropping.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of U-M item AGD2855.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/Level 1 TEI encoding of the title page and four-work selection on pages 1–59 of George H. McKnight's 1913 Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse. McKnight identifies Bodleian MS Digby 86 as the textual base for Dame Siriz and The Fox and Wolf in the Well, Bodleian MS Ashmole 61 as the base for Sir Cleges, and current NLS Adv.MS.19.1.11 as the source of Sir Cleges lines 40–42 and 130–132. He says that he collated the proofs against separate rotographic copies of those three witnesses, but does not identify the continuous transcription or compositor-copy route; no direct edition-to-rotograph or edition-to-codex transcription is asserted. He printed the Interludium de clerico et puella in a form closely dependent on Wilhelm Heuser's 1907 manuscript-based edition. Treichel's prior Sir Cleges parallel edition reaches Ashmole 61 through Max Kaluza's transcript checked by Karl D. Bülbring, while its Edinburgh column revises Henry Weber's 1810 manuscript edition through an unidentified correction route. The Interludium's unique British Library roll witness, Add MS 23986, is currently recorded as mislaid; a cataloged 2026 microfilm is requestable. Complete public facsimiles of Digby 86, Ashmole 61, and Adv.MS.19.1.11, plus complete public carriers for the used printed editions, are mapped to exact work-bearing portions and cached privately in full.

summaries.value: McKnight's encoded selection joins four anonymous Middle English verse works. Dame Siriz is a fabliau of clerical courtship, a go-between, and manufactured adultery; the fragmentary Interludium presents a clerk's courtship dialogue and mediation by an old woman; The Fox and Wolf in the Well is a beast fable of appetite and trickery; and Sir Cleges is a Christmas romance in which generosity brings poverty, miraculous cherries restore a displaced knight, and a comic reward of twelve blows exposes corrupt gatekeepers.

The source XML preserves the edition title page and the four work texts on printed pages 1–59, with McKnight's textual notes, while omitting the substantive preface, introduction, bibliography, explanatory notes, glossary, and other front and back matter. Dame Siriz and the Interludium are nested under one top-level poem DIV1, but the latter remains an independent work. The XML contains 1,406 L elements; the conventional total is 1,405 verse lines because the Interludium's Latin speaker formula is encoded as one additional L element.
